title: Anesthesia for Central Nervous System Diseases – MCQ
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav
section: Anesthesia for Central Nervous System Diseases

questions.question: What is the normal intracranial pressure (ICP) in a supine adult?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. 5–15 mmHg — Normal ICP in a supine adult ranges from 5–15 mmHg. Values above 20 mmHg are considered raised and require treatment.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Intracranial Pressure, Page 248

questions.question: The Monro-Kellie doctrine states that the cranial vault contains three components. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Bone marrow (10%) — The Monro-Kellie doctrine states the skull contains brain tissue (~80%), CSF (~10%), and blood (~10%). An increase in any one must be compensated by a decrease in another.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Monro-Kellie Doctrine, Page 248

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important determinant of cerebral blood flow (CBF)?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. PaCO2 — PaCO2 is the most potent physiological regulator of CBF. For every 1 mmHg change in PaCO2, CBF changes by approximately 1–2 mL/100g/min.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cerebral Blood Flow Regulation, Page 249

questions.question: Normal cerebral blood flow (CBF) in an adult is approximately:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. 50 mL/100g/min — Normal CBF is approximately 50 mL/100g/min, with grey matter receiving 80 mL/100g/min and white matter 20 mL/100g/min.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Normal CBF Values, Page 249

questions.question: Cerebral autoregulation maintains constant CBF over a MAP range of:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. 50–150 mmHg — Cerebral autoregulation operates between MAP of 50–150 mmHg. Below 50 mmHg, ischemia occurs; above 150 mmHg, breakthrough edema may result.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cerebral Autoregulation, Page 250

questions.question: At what PaO2 level does significant cerebral vasodilation begin?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Below 60 mmHg — Significant cerebral vasodilation occurs when PaO2 falls below 60 mmHg. Above this threshold, oxygen has minimal effect on CBF.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Effect of PaO2 on CBF, Page 250

questions.question: Which intravenous anesthetic agent causes the GREATEST reduction in cerebral metabolic rate (CMRO2)?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Thiopentone — Thiopentone (and other barbiturates) produce the greatest reduction in CMRO2, up to 55–60% reduction. They are used for cerebral protection ('barbiturate coma').
questions.topic: Effects of IV Agents on CBF
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: IV Anesthetics and CMRO2, Page 252

questions.question: Which intravenous anesthetic agent INCREASES cerebral blood flow and ICP?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Ketamine — Ketamine increases CMRO2, CBF, and ICP. It is generally avoided in patients with raised intracranial pressure.
questions.topic: Effects of IV Agents on CBF
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Ketamine and ICP, Page 252

questions.question: Among volatile anesthetic agents, which causes the LEAST increase in ICP?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Sevoflurane — Sevoflurane causes the least cerebral vasodilation among volatile agents and has minimal effect on ICP at concentrations <1 MAC. Halothane causes the most increase in ICP.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Inhalational Agents and ICP, Page 253

questions.question: The cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) is calculated as:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. MAP – ICP — CPP = MAP – ICP. Normal CPP is 70–80 mmHg. CPP below 50 mmHg may lead to cerebral ischemia.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cerebral Perfusion Pressure, Page 249

questions.question: What is the minimum acceptable CPP to avoid cerebral ischemia?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. 50 mmHg — CPP should be maintained above 50–60 mmHg to prevent cerebral ischemia. Below 50 mmHg, ischemic changes begin to occur.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: CPP Thresholds, Page 249

questions.question: The most effective method to rapidly reduce raised ICP during neurosurgery is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hyperventilation — Hyperventilation (reducing PaCO2 to 30–35 mmHg) causes cerebral vasoconstriction and is the fastest method to reduce ICP. However, its effect is transient (6–12 hours).
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Management of Raised ICP, Page 254

questions.question: The target PaCO2 during hyperventilation for ICP control is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. 30–35 mmHg — Target PaCO2 of 30–35 mmHg is recommended. Excessive hyperventilation (PaCO2 <25 mmHg) can cause cerebral ischemia due to excessive vasoconstriction.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Hyperventilation for ICP, Page 254

questions.question: What is the dose of mannitol used for ICP reduction?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. 0.25–1 g/kg — Mannitol is administered at 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 15–20 minutes. It acts as an osmotic diuretic, drawing water from brain tissue into the intravascular space.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Mannitol Therapy, Page 255

questions.question: Which muscle relaxant is CONTRAINDICATED in patients with raised ICP?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Succinylcholine — Succinylcholine can transiently increase ICP due to muscle fasciculations and its effect on cerebral vasculature. However, it may still be used for rapid sequence intubation when the benefit outweighs the risk.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Muscle Relaxants in Neurosurgery, Page 253

questions.question: The preferred induction agent for neurosurgical patients is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Propofol — Propofol is preferred because it reduces CMRO2, CBF, and ICP while maintaining cerebral autoregulation. It also provides smooth induction and rapid recovery.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Induction Agents, Page 252

questions.question: During craniotomy in the sitting position, the most feared complication is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Venous air embolism — Venous air embolism (VAE) is the most dangerous complication of sitting position craniotomy. Incidence can be as high as 25–50%. Air enters through open dural venous sinuses.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Sitting Position Craniotomy, Page 257

questions.question: The most sensitive monitor for detecting venous air embolism is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) — TEE is the most sensitive monitor for VAE, detecting as little as 0.02 mL/kg of air. Precordial Doppler is the most sensitive non-invasive monitor.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: VAE Monitoring, Page 258

questions.question: The most sensitive NON-INVASIVE monitor for venous air embolism is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Precordial Doppler — Precordial Doppler is the most sensitive non-invasive monitor for VAE, capable of detecting 0.05 mL/kg of air. It produces a characteristic 'mill-wheel' murmur.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: VAE Detection (Non-Invasive), Page 258

questions.question: In the event of venous air embolism, the IMMEDIATE first step is:

options.A: Place patient in left lateral decubitus position
options.B: Flood the surgical field with saline and cover with wet gauze
options.C: Aspirate air through central venous catheter

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Flood the surgical field with saline and cover with wet gauze — The immediate step is to inform the surgeon to flood the field with saline and apply bone wax to stop further air entry. Other measures follow: discontinue N2O, aspirate air via CVP, and left lateral/Trendelenburg position if hemodynamically unstable.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: VAE Treatment, Page 258

questions.question: Why should nitrous oxide (N2O) be avoided or discontinued during venous air embolism?

options.B: It expands the air bubble (34 times more soluble than nitrogen)

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. It expands the air bubble (34 times more soluble than nitrogen) — N2O is 34 times more soluble in blood than nitrogen. It rapidly diffuses into air bubbles, expanding them and worsening the hemodynamic effects of air embolism.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: N2O and Air Embolism, Page 259

questions.question: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score ranges from:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. 3 to 15 — GCS ranges from 3 (deep coma) to 15 (fully conscious). It assesses Eye opening (1–4), Verbal response (1–5), and Motor response (1–6).
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Glasgow Coma Scale, Page 256

questions.question: A GCS score of 8 or below indicates:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Severe head injury requiring intubation — GCS ≤8 = severe head injury and is an indication for endotracheal intubation to protect the airway. GCS 9–12 = moderate, GCS 13–15 = mild.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: GCS Classification, Page 256

questions.question: Which IV fluid is preferred in neurosurgical patients?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl) — Normal saline (0.9% NaCl, 308 mOsm/L) is the preferred fluid because it is slightly hyperosmolar and does not worsen cerebral edema. Glucose-containing fluids are avoided as hyperglycemia worsens ischemic brain injury.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Fluid Management in Neurosurgery, Page 255

questions.question: Why are glucose-containing IV fluids AVOIDED in neurosurgical patients?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hyperglycemia worsens ischemic neurological injury — Hyperglycemia promotes anaerobic glycolysis in ischemic brain tissue, leading to lactic acidosis and worsening neuronal injury. Blood glucose should be maintained below 180 mg/dL.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Glucose and Brain Injury, Page 255

questions.question: The brain receives what percentage of cardiac output?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. 15% — The brain receives approximately 15% of cardiac output (about 750 mL/min) despite constituting only 2% of body weight. It consumes approximately 20% of total body oxygen.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cerebral Blood Supply, Page 248

questions.question: Cerebral metabolic rate of oxygen (CMRO2) in an adult is approximately:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. 3.5 mL/100g/min — Normal CMRO2 is approximately 3.5 mL/100g/min. The ratio of CBF to CMRO2 (flow-metabolism coupling) is normally maintained at approximately 14:1.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: CMRO2, Page 249

questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST position for a patient with raised ICP?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Head elevated 15–30 degrees — Head-up position (15–30°) promotes venous drainage from the brain, reducing ICP. The head should be kept midline to avoid jugular venous compression.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Positioning for ICP, Page 254

questions.question: Cushing's triad (sign of severely raised ICP) consists of:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: A. Hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respiration — Cushing's triad consists of systemic hypertension, reflex bradycardia, and irregular (often slow) respiration. It is a late and ominous sign of brainstem compression.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cushing Response, Page 251

questions.question: Which volatile agent is most commonly used for maintenance of anesthesia in neurosurgery?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Sevoflurane — Sevoflurane is preferred because it has minimal effect on ICP at <1 MAC, preserves cerebral autoregulation better than other agents, and allows smooth induction and rapid emergence for neurological assessment.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Inhalational Agent Selection, Page 253

questions.question: In a patient with supratentorial tumor, the MOST common presenting symptom is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Headache — Headache (often morning headache that improves on standing) is the most common presenting symptom of supratentorial tumors, caused by raised ICP. Seizures are the second most common.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Supratentorial Tumors, Page 256

questions.question: Posterior fossa tumors are most commonly associated with which sign?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Cerebellar signs and raised ICP (hydrocephalus) — Posterior fossa tumors commonly present with cerebellar signs (ataxia, nystagmus, dysmetria) and raised ICP due to obstructive hydrocephalus from compression of the 4th ventricle.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Posterior Fossa Surgery, Page 257

questions.question: During posterior fossa surgery, stimulation of which cranial nerve can cause sudden bradycardia?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Vagus (X) — Surgical stimulation near the vagus nerve (CN X) or its nuclei in the floor of the 4th ventricle can cause sudden profound bradycardia or even asystole. Atropine should be readily available.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cranial Nerve Stimulation, Page 257

questions.question: Hypertonic saline used for ICP reduction is available in which concentration?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. All of the above — Hypertonic saline is available in various concentrations (1.8%, 3%, 7.5%, 23.4%). It is an alternative to mannitol for osmotic therapy in ICP reduction, especially in hypovolemic patients.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Hypertonic Saline, Page 255

questions.question: The advantage of hypertonic saline over mannitol for ICP reduction is:

options.A: It does not cause rebound increase in ICP
options.C: It does not cause electrolyte imbalance

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Both A and B — Unlike mannitol, hypertonic saline does not cause osmotic diuresis (so no hypovolemia) and has less rebound ICP elevation. It also augments intravascular volume, which is beneficial in trauma patients.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Hypertonic Saline vs Mannitol, Page 255

questions.question: Diabetes insipidus (DI) after pituitary surgery is characterized by:

options.A: Low urine output, high urine specific gravity
options.B: High urine output, low urine specific gravity (<1.005)
options.C: Low urine output, low urine specific gravity
options.D: Normal urine output, high serum sodium

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. High urine output, low urine specific gravity (<1.005) — Central DI presents with polyuria (urine output >250 mL/hr), dilute urine (specific gravity <1.005, osmolality <200 mOsm/kg), and rising serum sodium (hypernatremia). It results from ADH deficiency.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Pituitary Surgery - Diabetes Insipidus, Page 260

questions.question: The treatment of central diabetes insipidus after pituitary surgery is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Desmopressin (DDAVP) — Desmopressin (DDAVP), a synthetic ADH analog, is the treatment of choice for central DI. It can be given intranasally (10–40 mcg), IV, or subcutaneously (1–4 mcg).
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: DI Treatment, Page 260

questions.question: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) is most commonly graded using which scale?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hunt and Hess scale — The Hunt and Hess scale (Grades I–V) is the most commonly used clinical grading system for SAH severity, correlating with surgical risk and prognosis.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: SAH Grading, Page 261

questions.question: The most serious complication of aneurysmal SAH that occurs between days 4–14 is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Cerebral vasospasm — Cerebral vasospasm is the most serious delayed complication of SAH, peaking between days 4–14. It can lead to delayed ischemic neurological deficit (DIND) and infarction.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cerebral Vasospasm, Page 261

questions.question: Triple-H therapy for cerebral vasospasm after SAH includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Hypothermia — Triple-H therapy = Hypertension + Hypervolemia + Hemodilution. It aims to increase CBF through the vasospastic vessels. Hypothermia is not part of triple-H therapy.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Triple-H Therapy, Page 262

questions.question: Nimodipine is given after SAH because it:

options.B: Prevents cerebral vasospasm and improves neurological outcome

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Prevents cerebral vasospasm and improves neurological outcome — Nimodipine (a calcium channel blocker with cerebral selectivity) is given for 21 days after SAH. It has been shown to reduce the incidence of ischemic deficits and improve outcomes, though it may not prevent angiographic vasospasm.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Nimodipine in SAH, Page 262

questions.question: During aneurysm clipping, temporary clipping of the parent vessel is used for. What neuroprotective agent may be administered?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Thiopentone (burst suppression) — Thiopentone is administered to achieve burst suppression on EEG during temporary clipping, providing cerebral protection by reducing CMRO2 during the period of focal ischemia.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cerebral Protection, Page 263

questions.question: In traumatic brain injury (TBI), secondary brain injury is caused by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. The initial mechanical impact — The initial mechanical impact causes PRIMARY brain injury. Secondary brain injury is caused by systemic insults like hypotension (most detrimental), hypoxia, hypercarbia, hyperglycemia, hyperthermia, and seizures.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Primary vs Secondary Brain Injury, Page 264

questions.question: The SINGLE most detrimental systemic insult in traumatic brain injury is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hypotension — Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) is the single most detrimental secondary insult in TBI, doubling mortality. Even a single episode of hypotension significantly worsens outcome.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Secondary Insults in TBI, Page 264

questions.question: Awake craniotomy is indicated for surgery near:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Eloquent cortex (speech and motor areas) — Awake craniotomy allows intraoperative cortical mapping of eloquent areas (speech, motor, sensory cortex) to maximize tumor resection while preserving neurological function.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Awake Craniotomy, Page 265

questions.question: Lumbar CSF drain placement is contraindicated in:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: A. Supratentorial tumors with midline shift — Lumbar CSF drainage is contraindicated when there is supratentorial mass with midline shift, as sudden CSF removal from below can cause transtentorial (uncal) herniation.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: CSF Drainage Contraindications, Page 254

questions.question: Propofol infusion syndrome is characterized by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Hypernatremia — Propofol infusion syndrome (with prolonged high-dose infusion >48h, >4 mg/kg/hr) causes severe metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, renal failure, cardiac failure, and lipemia. Hypernatremia is not a feature.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Propofol Infusion Syndrome, Page 266

questions.question: Emergence from anesthesia in neurosurgery should ideally be:

options.B: Smooth, avoiding coughing and straining (to prevent ICP spikes)
options.C: Delayed by 24 hours in all cases
options.D: Preceded by reversal of all neuromuscular blockade with neostigmine

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Smooth, avoiding coughing and straining (to prevent ICP spikes) — Smooth emergence is critical in neurosurgery to avoid coughing, straining, and hypertension, all of which can raise ICP and cause postoperative bleeding. IV lidocaine (1.5 mg/kg) before extubation can help.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Emergence in Neurosurgery, Page 266

questions.question: The cerebral steal phenomenon occurs when:

options.A: A vasodilator diverts blood away from ischemic areas to normal areas
options.B: A vasoconstrictor shunts blood to ischemic areas
options.C: Hyperventilation improves perfusion to all brain regions equally
options.D: Mannitol selectively improves flow to ischemic brain

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: A. A vasodilator diverts blood away from ischemic areas to normal areas — Cerebral steal occurs when vasodilators (like volatile agents) dilate vessels in normal brain, diverting blood AWAY from ischemic regions (which have maximally dilated vessels that cannot dilate further). The inverse (Robin Hood) phenomenon redirects blood TO ischemic areas.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Cerebral Steal Phenomenon, Page 251

questions.question: Intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring during neurosurgery includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Electromyography of the diaphragm — Common neuromonitoring modalities include SSEP (sensory pathway), MEP (motor pathway), EEG (cortical activity), BAEP (brainstem auditory), and direct nerve stimulation. Diaphragm EMG is not a standard neurological monitor.
questions.reference: Ch 20, Section: Intraoperative Neuromonitoring, Page 267
